title: Car Accidents
description: Car accidents are the most common type of personal injury case in California and Arizona. Whether you were rear-ended, T-boned, or involved in a multi-vehicle collision, understanding your rights and next steps can protect your health and your claim.
metaTitle: Car Accident Guide — What To Do After a Car Crash
metaDescription: What to do after a car accident in California or Arizona. Get clear steps, evidence checklists, timeline risks, and guidance on when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Distracted Driving
commonCauses.description: Cell phone use, eating, or other distractions that take a driver's attention off the road.

commonCauses.title: Speeding
commonCauses.description: Driving over the posted speed limit or too fast for road conditions.

commonCauses.title: Drunk or Impaired Driving
commonCauses.description: Alcohol, drugs, or prescription medications that impair reaction time and judgment.

commonCauses.title: Running Red Lights or Stop Signs
commonCauses.description: Failing to obey traffic signals, often causing T-bone or intersection collisions.

commonCauses.title: Tailgating
commonCauses.description: Following too closely, leaving insufficient stopping distance.

commonCauses.title: Unsafe Lane Changes
commonCauses.description: Merging without checking blind spots or using turn signals.

commonCauses.title: Road Rage
commonCauses.description: Aggressive driving behaviors including cutting off, brake-checking, and deliberate collisions.

immediateSteps.title: Call 911 if anyone is injured
immediateSteps.description: If there are injuries — even minor ones — call 911 immediately. Do not move injured people unless there is an immediate danger.

immediateSteps.title: Move to safety if possible
immediateSteps.description: If the vehicles are drivable and blocking traffic, move them to the shoulder. Turn on hazard lights.

immediateSteps.title: Document the scene with photos
immediateSteps.description: Before moving anything, photograph all vehicles, damage, skid marks, road conditions, and any visible injuries.

immediateSteps.title: Exchange information with other driver(s)
immediateSteps.description: Get name, license number, insurance company, policy number, and contact information from all drivers involved.

immediateSteps.title: Get witness information
immediateSteps.description: Ask any bystanders for their name and phone number. Independent witnesses can be critical if fault is disputed.

immediateSteps.title: Seek medical evaluation within 24-48 hours
immediateSteps.description: Even if you feel fine, see a doctor. Delayed symptoms (whiplash, concussion) are common. A medical record establishes a connection between the accident and your injuries.

immediateSteps.title: Notify your insurance company
immediateSteps.description: Report the accident to your insurer promptly. Stick to the facts — avoid speculating about fault or the extent of injuries.

immediateSteps.title: Keep records of everything
immediateSteps.description: Save all medical bills, repair estimates, rental car receipts, and any documentation of lost wages.

items: Photos of all vehicle damage (all angles)
Photos of the accident location and road conditions
Photos of any visible injuries
Police report number
Other driver's license, insurance, and contact info
Names and contact info of witnesses
Emergency room or urgent care visit records
All physician and specialist notes
Diagnostic imaging reports (X-ray, MRI, CT scan)
Physical therapy records
Prescription receipts
Ongoing symptom journal
All medical bills and invoices
Vehicle repair estimates and invoices
Rental car receipts
Employer letter or pay stubs documenting missed work
Any out-of-pocket expense receipts related to the accident
Your own insurance policy documents
All correspondence with insurance adjusters (written and logged calls)
Any settlement offers received in writing
Copy of the police report (order once available)
Dashcam footage (yours or nearby traffic cameras)
Surveillance video from nearby businesses
Screenshots of any relevant social media posts
Weather reports for the day and location

timelineRisks.risk: Delayed injury symptoms may go undocumented
timelineRisks.action: Seek a medical evaluation even if you feel fine. Establish a medical record connecting the accident to any injuries.
Return to the accident location for additional photos if needed. Download dashcam footage before it overwrites.
Most policies require prompt reporting. Notify your insurer within the first week to avoid coverage issues.
Write a detailed account of the accident and your injuries while your memory is fresh. Collect witness statements.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: You generally have 2 years from the date of the accident to file a personal injury lawsuit in California. Missing this deadline typically bars your claim.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Arizona also has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims. Government vehicles may have shorter notice requirements.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Insurance adjusters may contact you within days with a settlement offer. Early offers are typically low and made before the full extent of injuries is known. You are not required to accept.
The other driver's insurer may ask you to give a recorded statement. You generally are not required to comply with the opposing insurer's requests. Consult with an attorney before agreeing.
Insurers may argue about who was at fault or assign shared blame to reduce their payout. California uses pure comparative fault — your recovery is reduced by your percentage of fault.
If the at-fault driver lacks sufficient insurance, your own uninsured/underinsured motorist (UM/UIM) coverage may apply. Review your policy carefully.
Some California policies include MedPay, which covers medical expenses regardless of fault. This can help cover immediate costs while your claim is being resolved.

whenToGetLawyer: You or any passenger sustained injuries requiring medical treatment
The other driver was uninsured or underinsured
Fault is disputed or you were partially blamed
You received a quick settlement offer before fully understanding your injuries
A commercial vehicle (truck, bus, rideshare) was involved
The accident involved a government vehicle or unsafe road conditions
An insurance adjuster is pressuring you to settle quickly
Your injuries have caused significant lost wages or long-term disability
